message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To estimate the impact of various mitigation strategies on COVID-19 transmission in a U.S. jail beyond those offered in national guidelines.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We developed a stochastic dynamic transmission model of COVID-19 in one large urban U.S. jail among staff and incarcerated individuals. We divided the outbreak into four intervention phases: the start of the outbreak, depopulation of the jail, increased proportion of people in single cells, and asymptomatic testing. We used the next generation method to estimate the basic reproduction ratio,
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    , in each phase. We estimated the fraction of new cases, hospitalizations, and deaths averted by these interventions along with the standard measures of sanitization, masking, and social distancing interventions.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    For the first outbreak phase, the estimated
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    was 8.23 (95% CrI: 5.01-12.90), and for the subsequent phases,
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      0,
                      <jats:italic>phase</jats:italic>
                      2
                    </jats:sub>
                    = 3.58 (95% CrI: 2.46-5.08),
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      0,
                      <jats:italic>phase</jats:italic>
                      3
                    </jats:sub>
                    = 1.72 (95% CrI: 1.41-2.12), and
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      0,
                      <jats:italic>phase</jats:italic>
                      4
                    </jats:sub>
                    = 0.45 (95% CrI: 0.32-0.59). In total, the jail’s interventions prevented approximately 83% of projected cases and hospitalizations and 89% of deaths over 83 days.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Depopulation, single celling, and asymptomatic testing within jails can be effective strategies to mitigate COVID-19 transmission in addition to standard public health measures.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Policy Implications</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Decision-makers should prioritize reductions in the jail population, single celling, and testing asymptomatic populations, as additional measures to manage COVID-19 within correctional settings.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: The effectiveness of interventions to reduce COVID-19 transmission in a large urban jail

reference.unstructured: Kaeble D , Cowhig, M. Correctional Populations in the United States. Washington, DC: U.S. Department of Justice, Bureau of Justice Statistics; 2018.
Hawks L , Woolhandler S , McCormick D. COVID-19 in prisons and jails in the United States. JAMA Intern Med 2020; Epub ahead of print.

reference.article-title: COVID-19 in correctional and detention facilities United States, February-April 2020
